name: Calapooia River & Ames Creek
short_description: BLM public rockhounding along the scenic Calapooia River where gentle slopes and creek access yield quality agates and jasper specimens. No permit required for personal collection.
description:
Calapooia River and Ames Creek offer accessible rockhounding opportunities on BLM public land in the Willamette Valley foothills. This low-elevation site features gentle terrain that makes it ideal for beginners and families wanting to explore rockhounding without technical difficulty or extensive hiking. The area benefits from stream erosion that constantly exposes new agate and jasper specimens in creek beds and along riverbanks.

The geology here reflects the Cascade Range's western slopes, where Eocene basaltic rocks and silica-rich volcanic materials were weathered and transported by water over millions of years. Chalcedony nodules and banded agates formed within ancient lava flows, and the river's ongoing erosion naturally exposes these specimens at ground level. The creek environment is ideal for finding waterworn specimens that have been naturally tumbled.

Collecting is straightforward - search the gravel bars, creek bottoms, and low banks where specimens accumulate. The gentle slope and good accessibility mean you can walk productive areas easily without scrambling or climbing. Spring and early summer offer the best conditions when water levels make gravel bars accessible but the area is not flooded.

This BLM location welcomes recreational collectors under standard personal use rules. The combination of easy access, scenic river setting, and consistent gem quality makes Calapooia River a reliable choice for Oregon rockhounders of any experience level.
history: The Calapooia River drainage sits within the western Cascade Range, where Eocene volcanic activity (roughly 50 million years ago) deposited thick sequences of basalt and silica-rich pyroclastic material. Subsequent weathering and river erosion have continuously exposed agate and jasper nodules that formed within these ancient volcanic deposits, making this drainage a productive natural collecting area for over a century of Oregon rockhounders.
tips: Search the inside curves of the creek where water slows and deposits heavier stones - agates and jasper specimens tend to accumulate here rather than on straight stretches. Look for translucent or banded stones with characteristic waxy luster; agates often show white, tan, or rust-red banding. Bring a small garden pick or flathead screwdriver to work loose specimens from gravel banks - avoid pulling rocks from active stream flow. Check the creek bed after winter runoff when new material is exposed.
terrain_notes: Elevation 530 feet with gentle slopes throughout the area. The terrain transitions from forested upland to open gravel bars and low banks along the river. Walking is easy on gravel and open ground; footing is generally stable with no steep drops. Creek access is straightforward, though water depth and flow vary seasonally. Vegetation is light to moderate along the river corridor.
best_season: April - October, with peak conditions May - September. Spring (April-May) brings higher water that exposes new gravel; early summer (June-July) offers dry conditions ideal for creek walking. Avoid November - March when water levels rise significantly and weather is wet and unpredictable.
written_directions: From Holley, head east on OR-228 toward the Calapooia River drainage for approximately 3 miles. Look for BLM access points marked along the river where pullouts allow parking near productive gravel bars. The river is visible from OR-228 in several locations. Most productive collecting areas are within 0.25 miles of the highway on foot, making access very convenient.
road_conditions: OR-228 is paved highway. Access to specific collecting areas via short walking distance from pullouts along the highway.
parking_notes: Multiple small pullouts along OR-228 near the river. Parking is informal BLM access parking - capacity 2-4 vehicles per pullout. No developed lot, but adequate space for casual rockhunters.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collect from creek beds and gravel bars only; do not remove specimens from active bedrock or excavate beyond casual surface collection.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized equipment
No power tools or active excavation
Leave gates as you find them
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Holley has limited services; Sweet Creek area (5 miles) and Corvallis (15 miles) offer gas, food, and water.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat and open terrain with gentle slopes accessible to most hikers. Unpaved areas are gravel and stable. No wheelchair access to creek areas due to uneven ground, but pullouts are accessible. Sturdy shoes recommended for creek walking.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find at Calapooia River and Ames Creek in Oregon?
faq.answer: This BLM site is known for quality agate and jasper specimens, along with chalcedony and quartz. The agates often show attractive banding in white, tan, and rust-red colors. Specimens are typically found as water-worn stones in creek gravel and along low riverbanks, making them accessible to beginners.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Calapooia River near Holley, Oregon?
faq.answer: No permit is required. This is BLM public land open for recreational collecting under personal use rules. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day without any paperwork or fees - making it perfect for casual rockhounders.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to find agate at Calapooia River, Oregon?
faq.answer: May through September offers the best conditions for collecting. Spring (April-May) brings runoff that exposes fresh material, while early summer (June-July) provides dry weather ideal for creek walking and gravel bar access. Avoid winter months when water levels rise and the area becomes muddy.

faq.question: How far is rockhounding at Calapooia River from Holley, Oregon?
faq.answer: The collecting areas are only 3 miles from Holley and directly accessible from OR-228. Most productive spots are within a short 0.25-mile walk from roadside pullouts, making this one of Oregon's most convenient rockhounding locations.

faq.question: Is Calapooia River good for beginner rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes, it is ideal for beginners and families. The terrain is gentle with easy walking, no climbing required, and specimens are found at ground level in creek beds. Kids as young as 6 can participate, and the friendly BLM rules allow casual surface collection with no special equipment needed.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect at Calapooia River in Oregon?
faq.answer: A small garden pick or flathead screwdriver is helpful for working specimens loose from gravel banks. Most collecting here is casual surface picking from creek beds, so even novice rockhounders can be successful without specialized tools. Wear sturdy shoes for creek walking.

meta_description: Find agates, jasper & chalcedony at Calapooia River, Oregon. Free BLM public land, no permit required. Easy access near Holley, perfect for families.
